links.text: Science and Senior Course Level Requirements

sections.heading: MATH 117 - Honors Calculus I
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 4-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: first term  Functions, continuity, and the derivative. Applications of the derivative. Extended limits and L’Hospital’s rule. Prerequisites: Mathematics 30-1 and Mathematics 31, or consent of the Department. Notes: (1) This course is designed for students with at least 80 percent in Pure Mathematics 30 or Mathematics 30-1 and Mathematics 31. (2) Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 100, 113, 114, 117, 134, 144, 154 or SCI 100. (3) Engineering students will receive a weight of 4.0 units for this course.

sections.heading: MATH 216 - Introduction to Analysis
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Sets and functions. Induction. Axiomatic introduction of the real numbers. Sequences and series. Continuity and properties of continuous functions. Differentiation. Riemann integral. Corequisite: One of MATH 101, 115, 136, 146, 156 or SCI 100. Note: This course may not be taken for credit if credit has already been obtained in MATH 117. Credit can only be obtained in one of MATH 216 or MATH 314.

sections.heading: MATH 100 - Calculus for Engineering I
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3.5  Approved Hours: 3-0-1  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Review of numbers, inequalities, functions, analytic geometry; limits, continuity; derivatives and applications, Taylor polynomials; log, exp, and inverse trig functions. Integration, fundamental theorem of calculus substitution, trapezoidal and Simpson’s rules. Prerequisites: Mathematics 30-1 and Mathematics 31. Notes: (1) Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 100, 113, 114, 117, 134, 144, 154, or SCI 100. (2) Students in all sections of this course will write a common final examination. (3) Restricted to Engineering students. Non-Engineering students who take this course will receive 3 units.

title: MATH 134 - Calculus for the Life Sciences I

sections.heading: MATH 134 - Calculus for the Life Sciences I
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  The derivative as a rate of change. Differentiation of elementary, trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions. The definite integral as a summation. Integration. The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Applications in the context of the life sciences. Prerequisite: Mathematics 30-1. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 100, 113, 114, 117, 134, 144, 154 or SCI 100.

title: MATH 144 - Calculus for the Mathematical and Physical Sciences I

sections.heading: MATH 144 - Calculus for the Mathematical and Physical Sciences I
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  The derivative as a rate of change. Differentiation of elementary, trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions. The definite integral as a summation. Integration. The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Taylor polynomials. Applications in the context of the physical sciences. Prerequisite: Mathematics 30-1. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one MATH 100, 113, 114, 117, 134, 144, 154 or SCI 100.

title: MATH 154 - Calculus for Business and Economics I

sections.heading: MATH 154 - Calculus for Business and Economics I
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  The derivative as a rate of change. Differentiation of elementary, trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions. The definite integral as a summation. Integration. The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Optimization. Applications in the context of business and economics. Prerequisite: Mathematics 30-1. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 100, 113, 114, 117, 134, 144, 154 or SCI 100.

sections.heading: MATH 101 - Calculus for Engineering II
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3.5  Approved Hours: 3-0-1  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Area between curves, techniques of integration. Applications of integration to planar areas and lengths, volumes and masses. First order ordinary differential equations: separable, linear, direction fields, Euler’s method, applications. Infinite series, power series, Taylor expansions with remainder terms. Polar coordinates. Rectangular, spherical and cylindrical coordinates in 3-dimensional space. Parametric curves in the plane and space: graphing, arc length, curvature; normal binormal, tangent plane in 3- dimensional space. Volumes and surface areas of rotation. Prerequisite: MATH 100. Notes: (1) Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 101, 115, 118, 136, 146, 156 or SCI 100. (2) Students in all sections of this course will write a common final examination. (3) Restricted to Engineering students. Non-Engineering students who take this course will receive 3 units.

sections.heading: MATH 118 - Honors Calculus II
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 4-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: second term  Integration and the Fundamental Theorem. Techniques and applications of integration. Derivatives and integrals of the exponential, and trigonometric functions. Introduction to infinite series. Introduction to partial derivatives. Prerequisite: MATH 117. (Students with a 100-level calculus course different from MATH 117 may be admitted with consent of the Department.) Notes: (1) Credit can be obtained in at most one MATH 101, 115, 118, 136, 146, 156 or SCI 100. (2) Engineering students will receive a weight of 4.0 units for this course.

title: MATH 136 - Calculus for the Life Sciences II

sections.heading: MATH 136 - Calculus for the Life Sciences II
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Techniques and applications of integration. Improper integrals. Differential equations and mathematical modelling. Partial differentiation. Applications in the context of the life sciences. Prerequisite: One of MATH 100, 113, 114, 117, 134, 144 or 154. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 101, 115, 118, 136, 146, 156 or SCI 100.

title: MATH 146 - Calculus for the Mathematical and Physical Sciences II

sections.heading: MATH 146 - Calculus for the Mathematical and Physical Sciences II
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Techniques and applications of integration. Improper integrals. Introduction to differential equations. Partial differentiation. Applications in the context of the physical sciences. Prerequisite: One of MATH 100, 113, 114, 117, 134, 144 or 154. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 101, 115, 118, 136, 146, 156 or SCI 100.

title: MATH 156 - Calculus for Business and Economics II

sections.heading: MATH 156 - Calculus for Business and Economics II
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Techniques and applications of integration. Improper integrals. Partial differentiation. Multivariate optimization. Probability and calculus. Applications in the context of business and economics. Prerequisite: One of MATH 100, 113, 114, 117, 134, 144 or 154. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 101, 115, 118, 136, 146, 156 or SCI 100.

sections.heading: MATH 102 - Applied Linear Algebra
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3.5  Approved Hours: 3-0-1  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Vectors and matrices, solution of linear equations, equations of lines and planes, determinants, matrix algebra, orthogonality and applications (Gram-Schmidt), eigenvalues and eigenvectors and applications, complex numbers. Prerequisite or corequisite: MATH 100. Notes: (1) Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 102, 125, or 127. (2) Students in all sections of this course will write a common final examination. (3) Restricted to Engineering students. Non-Engineering students who take this course will receive 3 units.

sections.heading: MATH 125 - Linear Algebra I
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Systems of linear equations. Vectors in n-space, vector equations of lines and planes. Matrix algebra, inverses and invertibility. Introduction to linear transformations. Subspaces of n-space. Determinants. Introduction to eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Complex numbers. Dot product, cross product and orthogonality. Applications in a variety of fields. Prerequisite: Mathematics 30-1. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 102, 125 or 127.

sections.heading: MATH 127 - Honors Linear Algebra I
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 4-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: first term  Linear equations; Euclidean spaces, matrices. Complex numbers and fields. Vector spaces : basis, dimension, linear transformations. Introductions to groups and rings; permutation groups. Determinants. Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors. Prerequisite: Mathematics 30-1. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one of Math 102, 125 or 127.

sections.heading: MATH 209 - Calculus for Engineering III
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3.5  Approved Hours: 3-0-1  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Partial differentiation, derivatives of integrals. Multiple integration using rectangular, cylindrical, and spherical coordinates. Vector Field Theory. Prerequisite: MATH 101. Prerequisite or corequisite: MATH 102. Notes: (1) This course may not be taken for credit if credit has already been obtained in MATH 215, MATH 315, MATH 317 or MA PH 351. (2) Students in all sections of this course will write a common final examination. (3) Restricted to Engineering students. Non-Engineering students who take this course will receive 3 units.

sections.heading: MATH 214 - Calculus III
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Sequences and series, convergence tests, and Taylor series. Curves, tangent vectors, and arc length. Applications of partial differentiation. Polar, cylindrical, and spherical coordinates. Multiple integration. Prerequisite: One of MATH 101, 115, 118, 136, 146 or 156. One of MATH 102, 125 or 127 recommended. Note: This course may not be taken for credit if credit has already been obtained in MATH 209 or 217.

sections.heading: MATH 217 - Honors Calculus III
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 4-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: first term  Axiomatic development of the real number system. Topology of Rn. Sequences, limits and continuity. Multi-variable calculus: differentiation and integration, including integration in spherical and polar coordinates. The differential and the chain rule. Taylor’s Formula, maxima and minima. Introduction to vector field theory. Prerequisites: One of MATH 102, 125 or 127, and either MATH 118 or MATH 216. Notes: (1) MATH 216 may be accepted as corequisite with consent of the Department. (2) Engineering students will receive a weight of 4.0 units for this course.

sections.heading: MATH 160 - Higher Arithmetic
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Elementary Number Theory, Numeration Systems, Number Systems and Elementary Probability Theory. Math Fair. Prerequisite: Mathematics 30-1 or 30-2, or consent of Department. Notes: (1) This course is restricted to Elementary Education students. (2) This course cannot be used for credit towards a Science degree.

sections.heading: MATH 260 - Mathematical Reasoning for Teachers
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Reasoning and problem solving in the context of logic, algebra, geometry, and combinatorics. Prerequisite: MATH 160, or consent of Department. Notes: (1) This course is restricted to Elementary Education students. (2) This course cannot be used for credit towards a Science degree.

sections.heading: MATH 253 - Theory of Interest
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Accumulation and amount functions, effective, nominal, simple, and compound rates, force of interest and discount, simple and general annuities certain, variable annuities and perpetuities, amortization schedules and sinking funds, bonds and other securities, applications, installment loans, depreciation, depletion, capitalized cost. Prerequisite: One of MATH 101, 115, 118, 136, 146, 156 or SCI 100. Corequisite: MATH 209 or 214.

sections.heading: MATH 111 - Introduction to Discrete Mathematics
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  A problem-solving approach to discrete mathematics, covering secret codes, public-key codes, error-correcting codes, enumeration, recurrence relations, induction, graph theory, graph algorithms and parallel algorithms. Prerequisite: MATH 30-1, 30-2, or equivalent. Note : Credit can only be obtained in at most one of MATH 111 or MATH 222.

sections.heading: MATH 243 - Transformation Geometry
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: second term  Transformation geometry, isometry and homothety, applications in Euclidean geometry; the algebra of transformations, the Classification Theorem, frieze patterns and wall-paper groups. Prerequisite: MATH 241

title: STAT 151 - Introduction to Applied Statistics I

sections.heading: STAT 151 - Introduction to Applied Statistics I
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Data collection and presentation, descriptive statistics. Probability distributions, sampling distributions and the central limit theorem. Point estimation, confidence intervals and hypothesis testing. Correlation and regression analysis. ANOVA. Goodness of fit and contingency table. Prerequisite: Mathematics 30-1 or 30-2. Notes: (1) Credit can be obtained in at most one of STAT 151, STAT 161, and STAT 235. (2) This course may not be taken for credit if credit has been obtained in STAT 222, STAT 266, STAT 276, KIN 109, PEDS 109, PSYCH 211, PTHER 352, SCI 151 or SOC 210.

title: STAT 161 - Introductory Statistics for Business and Economics

sections.heading: STAT 161 - Introductory Statistics for Business and Economics
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Data collection and presentation, descriptive statistics. Probability distributions, sampling distributions and the central limit theorem. Point estimation, confidence intervals and hypothesis testing. Correlation and regression analysis. ANOVA. Goodness of fit and contingency table. Use of a microcomputer software package for statistical analyses in business and economics. Prerequisite: Mathematics 30-1 or 30-2. Notes: (1) Credit can be obtained in at most one of STAT 151, STAT 161, and STAT 235. (2) This course may not be taken for credit if credit has been obtained in obtained in STAT 222, STAT 266, STAT 276, KIN 109, PEDS 109, PSYCH 211, PTHER 352, SCI 151 or SOC 210.

links.text: Science and Senior Course Level Requirements

sections.heading: STAT 265 - Probability and Statistics I
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: first term  Sample space, events, combinatorial probability, conditional probability, independent events, Bayes Theorem, random variables, discrete random variables, expected values, moment generating function, inequalities, continuous distributions, multivariate distributions, independence. Corequisite: One of MATH 209, 214 or 217. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most two of STAT 181, STAT 281, or STAT 265.

sections.heading: STAT 266 - Probability and Statistics II
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: second term  Functions of random variables, sampling distributions, Central Limit Theorem, law of large numbers, statistical models for the data, likelihood, parameters and their interpretation, objectives of statistical inference, point and interval estimation, method of moments, basic notions of testing of hypotheses, errors of the first and second kind, significance level, power, p-value. Prerequisites: one of MATH 209, MATH 214, or MATH 217 and one of STAT 265 or STAT 281. Corequisites: One of MATH 225 or 227. Credit can only be obtained in one of STAT 266 or STAT 276.

sections.heading: MATH 227 - Honors Linear Algebra II
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 4-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: second term  Quotients and direct sums. Cayley-Hamilton. Canonical Forms (diagonal and Jordan). Real and Complex inner product spaces : orthogonality, singular value decomposition. Introduction to abstract algebra : groups, rings, and modules. Homomorphisms. Prerequisite: MATH 127. 127; Students with MATH 102 or 125 require MATH 226 as a co-requisite and consent of the Department. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 225 or 227.

sections.heading: MATH 226 - Algebraic Structures
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Groups and their homomorphisms; commutative rings and modules; fields and vector spaces; subgroups and quotient groups, permutation groups; modules, submodules, quotient modules; polynomials rings and their ideals, modules over polynomial rings. Prerequisite: MATH 125. Note: Cannot be taken for credit if credit has been received in MATH 227.

sections.heading: MATH 315 - Calculus IV
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Vector calculus. Line and surface integrals. The divergence, Green’s, and Stokes’ theorems. Differential forms. Prerequisite: One of MATH 102, 125 or 127, and either MATH 214 or MATH 217. Notes: Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 215 and MATH 315. This course may not be taken for credit if credit has already been obtained in MATH 209 or 317.

sections.heading: MATH 201 - Differential Equations
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3.5  Approved Hours: 3-0-1  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term or Spring/Summer  First-order equations; second-order linear equations: reduction of order, variation of parameters; Laplace transform; linear systems; power series; solution by series; separation of variables for PDEs. Prerequisite or corequisite: MATH 209 or 214. Notes: (1) Open only to students in Engineering and Specialization Geophysics. (2) Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 201, MATH 334, MATH 336, or MA PH 251. (3) Students in all sections of this course will write a common final examination. Non-Engineering students who take this course will receive 3 units.

sections.heading: MATH 334 - Ordinary Differential Equations
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  First order equations, linear equations of higher order. Power series solution. Laplace transform methods. Introduction to special functions. Introduction to linear systems. Prerequisites: One of MATH 102, 125 or 127, and one of MATH 209, 214 or 217. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 201, MATH 334, MATH 336, or MA PH 251.

sections.heading: MATH 336 - Honors Ordinary Differential Equations
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  First order differential equations. Linear systems of differential equations and linear differential equations of higher order. Stability and qualitative theory of 2-dimensional linear and non-linear systems. Laplace transform methods. Existences and uniqueness theorems. Prerequisites: MATH 225 or 227, and either MATH 209, 217, 314 or both 214 and 216. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 201, MATH 334, MATH 336, and MA PH 251.

sections.heading: MATH 411 - Honors Complex Variables
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: first term  Complex number system. Analytic functions. Cauchy’s Integral theorem and formula. Applications including the maximum modulus principle, Taylor expansion and Laurent expansion. Harmonic functions. The residue theorem with applications; calculus of residues, argument principle, and Rouche’s theorem. Basics of analytic continuation. Additional topics at the instructor’s discretion such as: Normal families, The Riemann mapping Theorem, Picard’s Theorem. Prerequisite: MATH 314 or 317. Notes: (1) This course is primarily for Honors students in Mathematics or Physics. (2) Offered in alternate years. It may be offered in intervening years if demand is sufficient.

sections.heading: MATH 448 - Introduction to Differential Geometry
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Riemannian geometry of n-space, metric tensors, various curvature concepts and their relationships, covariant differentiation, geodesics, parallel transport. Additional topics at the discretion of the instructor. Prerequisite: MATH 348, or MATH 217 and one of MATH 225 or 227. Note: Offered in alternate years. It may be offered in intervening years if demand is sufficient.

title: MATH 337 - Introduction to Partial Differential Equations

sections.heading: MATH 337 - Introduction to Partial Differential Equations
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Boundary value problems of classical Math Physics, orthogonal expansions, classical special functions. Advanced transform techniques. Prerequisites: One of MATH 209, 215, 217, 315 or MA PH 351, and one of MATH 201, MATH 334, MATH 336, or MA PH 251. Notes: (1) Credit can be obtained in at most one of MATH 300 or 337. (2) Course cannot be taken for credit if credit has been obtained in ECE 341.

title: MATH 311 - Theory of Functions of a Complex Variable

sections.heading: MATH 311 - Theory of Functions of a Complex Variable
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Complex numbers. Complex series. Functions of a complex variable. Cauchy’s theorem and contour integration. Residue Theorem and its applications. Corequisite: MATH 215 or MATH 315 or MATH 317 or MA PH 351. Note: This course may not be taken for credit if credit has already been obtained in MATH 309 or 411.

title: STAT 252 - Introduction to Applied Statistics II

sections.heading: STAT 252 - Introduction to Applied Statistics II
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Methods in applied statistics including regression techniques, analysis of variance and covariance, and methods of data analysis. Applications are taken from Biological, Physical and Social Sciences, and Business. Prerequisite: One of STAT 141, 151, 161, 235 or SCI 151. Notes: (1) Credit can be obtained in at most one of STAT 252, 319, 337 or 341, or AREC 313. (2) This course may not be taken for credit if credit has already been obtained in STAT 368 or 378.

sections.heading: MATH 447 - Elementary Topology
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  General point-set topology. Compactness, Tychonoff’s tbeorem, connectedness. Metric spaces, completeness, Baire’s theorem. Urysohn’s lemma. Topological manifolds. Homotopy theory, fundamental group, covering spaces. Prerequisite : MATH 216 or 217. Corequisites: MATH 328 or MA PH 464. Offered in alternate years. It may be offered in intervening years if demand is sufficient.

sections.heading: General Program
sections.content: The BSc General program is designed to provide students with a diversified education in more than one branch of study and includes a major and a minor subject or area of concentration. In addition to providing for a BSc General Degree, this allows for subsequent transfer to Specialization and Honors programs, at the Faculty of Science, for which there is no first-year admission. Students who are enrolled in the General program and who intend to transfer to another program should consult the appropriate Calendar description for the particular program of interest and carefully select their first-year core courses in accordance with the requirements of the specific program. It should be noted that this program requires that students include Faculty of Science courses that are offered only in English. See Chart 6.

sections.heading: Year 1
sections.content: 0 units to 6 units Elective  6 units to 12 units core French (see Classification of Courses and French Courses ) (see Note 1)  15 units to 18 units Science (see Note 3)

sections.heading: Year 2
sections.content: ANGL (6 units) ou ALS (6 units) (see Note 2)  18 units to 21 units Science (see Note 3)  0 units to 3 units core French (see Classification of Courses and French Courses ) (see Note 1)  FRANC 232 - Techniques de rédaction scientifique

sections.heading: Year 3
sections.content: 18 units Science  3 units Elective at the 200-level or higher  9 units Elective

sections.heading: Year 4
sections.content: 18 units Science  12 units Elective

sections.heading: Notes
sections.content: Students enrolled in the Faculté Saint-Jean are placed in French courses according to their previously completed program of study (see French Courses ). They are required to take FRANC 226 .  Students normally fulfill the English course requirement by completing either ANGL (6 units) or ALS (6 units). The ALS course is reserved for students enrolled in a Faculté Saint-Jean program who have not successfully completed English 30, English Language Arts 30-1, or an equivalent.  Students must complete 36 units in sciences during the first two years of their program.

sections.heading: Program Requirements
sections.content: To obtain a BSc General Degree, students must receive credit in 120 units. At least 72 units and not more than 90 units must be in Science. At least 30 units and not more than 48 units must be in Arts among which FRANC 232 is required.  The General Program is characterized by a core of courses, which must include the following: FRANC 232 . 6 units from among junior courses in English, ANGL or ENGL. 6 units from among junior courses in Computing Science or Mathematics and Statistics. 6 units from among junior courses in Astronomy, Chemistry, or Physics. 6 units from among junior courses in Biology or Psychology (Science). Note: BIOLE 107 and BIOLE 108 cannot be used for the minor in Agriculture.  FRANC 232 .  6 units from among junior courses in English, ANGL or ENGL.  6 units from among junior courses in Computing Science or Mathematics and Statistics.  6 units from among junior courses in Astronomy, Chemistry, or Physics.  6 units from among junior courses in Biology or Psychology (Science). Note: BIOLE 107 and BIOLE 108 cannot be used for the minor in Agriculture.  Normally, at least 30 units at the junior level must be successfully completed before students may register in senior-level courses.  Each student must complete a major subject area of concentration in Science. See below for specific course requirements in each major subject area of concentration in sciences. A minimum of 36 units and a maximum of 48 units are required in the major subject or area of concentration, with no more than 18 units at the junior level. Students must successfully complete a minimum of 12 units at the 300-level in the major.  Each student must also either: complete a minor subject or area of concentration in sciences (see list below). At least 24 units and not more than 36 units are required in the minor, with no more than 12 units at the junior level. Students must also successfully complete a minimum of 6 units at the 300-level in the minor. Note: For Non-Sciences Minors (see Section Non-Science Minors) complete a second Science major: Students who complete a second Science major will not have a minor. To achieve a double major in the BSc, students must complete at least one of the majors and at least 60 units at Faculté Saint-Jean. The Double Majors will be recorded on the student transcript.  complete a minor subject or area of concentration in sciences (see list below). At least 24 units and not more than 36 units are required in the minor, with no more than 12 units at the junior level. Students must also successfully complete a minimum of 6 units at the 300-level in the minor. Note: For Non-Sciences Minors (see Section Non-Science Minors)  complete a second Science major: Students who complete a second Science major will not have a minor. To achieve a double major in the BSc, students must complete at least one of the majors and at least 60 units at Faculté Saint-Jean. The Double Majors will be recorded on the student transcript.  The major and minor may not share courses from the same subject or area of concentration. The following combinations are not allowed:  Psychology (Science)/Psychology (Arts)  Courses in a major or a minor may not overlap. For example, if the major is the Physical Sciences, and minor is Chemistry, the major may be made up of Astronomy, Biochemistry, Geophysics, Mathematical Physics and Physics courses, but no Chemistry courses. The minor would consist exclusively of Chemistry courses.  Subject to receiving written approval from the Faculté Saint-Jean prior to registration, a maximum of 12 units may be taken from Faculties other than Science or Arts. For applicants to the BSc General who have already taken courses from Faculties other than Science or Arts, potential transfer credit for such courses will be assessed at the time of admission to the program. Such subjects are not included as part of the major or minor subject or area of concentration, nor toward the minimum requirement of 18 units in Arts, nor toward the minimum requirement of 72 units in Science.  Note: Course Listings contains a list of courses from other Faculties that are regarded as Science courses. These courses may be used toward the major and minor and do count toward the minimum requirement of 72 units in Science.

sections.heading: Majors
sections.content: A major area of concentration consists of courses taken from seven subject areas of concentration:

sections.heading: Biological Sciences (see Note 1)
sections.content: A major in the Biological Sciences consists of at least 36 units with at least 12 units at the 300-level or higher. The major must include the following:  At least 9 units in courses at the 200-level or higher with a lab component and offered by the Department of Biological Sciences. The 3 units from BIOLE 207 or BIOLE 208 may not be used to fulfill the program requirement.  At least 3 units from each of the following three areas of study:  Ecology, evolution or diversity  Genetics and molecular (or micro-) biology  Physiology, cell and developmental biology, anatomy  Consult departmental website for a list of approved courses for each of the three areas of study. BIOLE 107 , BIOLE 108 , BIOLE 207 and BIOLE 208 may not be used to fulfill the program requirements in a, b, or c.  At least 12 units at the 300-level or higher, of which at least 3 units must be at the 400-level. Many of the senior Biological Sciences courses require either BIOLE 207 or BIOLE 208 as a prerequisite so both courses are highly recommended.

sections.heading: Chemistry
sections.content: A major in Chemistry consists of at least 36 units with at least 12 units at the 300-level or higher. The major must include the following:  At least 12 units in CHIM at the 300-level or higher, of which at least 3 units must be at the 400-level  Any additional courses required to meet the minimum 36 units may come from CHIM or BIOCM.  Although it does not count toward the major, students completing a Chemistry major are recommended to take MATHQ 114 and MATHQ 115 . Some 200-level or higher CHIM courses require MATHQ 115 as a prerequisite, so students must plan accordingly.

sections.heading: Mathematical Sciences (see Note 2)
sections.content: A major in Mathematical Sciences consists of at least 36 units with at least 12 units at the 300-level or higher. The major must include the following:  At least 18 units at the 200-level or higher in MATHQ  At least 12 units at the 300-level or higher in MATHQ  Any additional courses required to meet the minimum 36 units may come from CMPUT, MATHQ or MATH, STATQ or STAT

sections.heading: Mathematics
sections.content: A major in Mathematics consists of at least 36 units with at least 12 units at the 300-level or higher. The major must include the following:  At least 12 units in MATHQ at the 300-level or higher, of which at least 3 units must be at the 400-level. If taken to fulfill this requirement, MATHQ 334 may count toward it.

sections.heading: Physical Sciences (see Note 3)
sections.content: A major in Physical Sciences consists of at least 42 units with at least 12 units at the 300-level or higher. The major must include the following:  At least 12 units at the 300-level or higher  At least 12 units in Chemistry and Physics courses  Chemistry courses may be chosen from BIOCM (see Note 4) or CHIM, and Physics courses may be chosen from ASTRO, GEOPH, MA PH (see Note 5), PHYSQ or PHYS. Many of the courses have MATHQ or MATH pre- or corequisites so students must plan accordingly.

sections.heading: Physics
sections.content: A major in Physics consists of at least 36 units with at least 12 units at the 300- level or higher. The major must include the following:  An additional 9 units at the 300-level or higher  Courses may be chosen from ASTRO, GEOPH, MA PH, PHYSQ or PHYS. Many of the courses have MATHQ or MATH pre- or corequisites so students must plan accordingly and might wish to pair the Physics major with a minor in Mathematics.

sections.heading: Science Psychology
sections.content: A major in Psychology consists of at least 36 units with at least 12 units at the 300-level or higher. The major must include the following:  At least 6 units in PSYCE at the 300-level or higher (minimum of 3 units from Science and 3 units from Arts)  At least 6 units in PSYCE at the 400-level or higher (minimum of 3 units from Science and 3 units from Arts)

sections.heading: Minors
sections.content: Students may choose a minor concentration either in Science or in Arts. However, the requirements of these routes contain important differences.  A Science minor consists of Science courses taken from one of the following subject areas of concentration:

sections.heading: Biological Sciences (see Note 1)
sections.content: A minor in the Biological Sciences consists of at least 24 units with at least 6 units at the 300-level or higher. The minor must include the following:  At least 3 units from each of the following three areas of study:  Ecology, evolution or diversity  Genetics and molecular (or micro-) biology  Physiology, cell and developmental biology, anatomy  Consult departmental website for a list of approved courses for each of the three areas of study. BIOLE 107 , BIOLE 108 , BIOLE 207 and BIOLE 208 and may not be used to fulfill the program requirements in a, b, or c.  At least 6 units at the 300-level or higher. Many of the 200-level or higher Biological Sciences courses require either BIOLE 207 or BIOLE 208 as a prerequisite so both courses are highly recommended.

sections.heading: Chemistry
sections.content: A minor in Chemistry consists of at least 24 units with at least 6 units at the 300-level or higher. The minor must include the following:  At least 6 units in CHIM or CHEM at the 300-level or higher  Any additional courses required to meet the minimum 24 units may come from CHIM or CHEM or BIOCM or BIOCH

sections.heading: Mathematical Sciences (see Note 2)
sections.content: A minor in Mathematical Sciences consists of at least 24 units with at least 6 units at the 300-level or higher. The minor must include the following:  At least 12 units at the 200-level or higher in MATHQ  At least 6 units at the 300-level or higher in MATHQ  Any additional courses required to meet the minimum 24 units may come from CMPUT, MATHQ or MATH, STATQ or STAT

sections.heading: Mathematics
sections.content: A minor in Mathematics consists of at least 24 units with at least 6 units at the 300-level or higher. The minor must include the following:  At least 6 units in MATHQ at the 300-level or higher

sections.heading: Physical Sciences (see Note 3)
sections.content: A minor in Physical Sciences consists of at least 27 units with at least 6 units at the 300-level or higher. The minor must include the following:  At least 9 units in Chemistry and Physics courses  At least 6 units at 300-level or higher.  Courses may be chosen from ASTRO, BIOCM, BIOCH (see Note 4), CHIM or CHEM, GEOPH, MA PH (see Note 5), PHYSQ or PHYS.

sections.heading: Physics
sections.content: A minor in Physics consists of at least 24 units with at least 6 units at the 300- level or higher. The minor must include the following:  An additional 3 units at the 300-level or higher  Courses may be chosen from ASTRO, GEOPH, MA PH, PHYSQ or PHYS. Many of the courses have MATH pre- or corequisites so students must plan accordingly and might wish to pair the Physics minor with a major in Mathematics.

sections.heading: Psychology
sections.content: A minor in Psychology consists of at least 24 units with at least 6 units at the 300-level or higher. The minor must include the following:  At least 6 units in PSYCE at the 300-level or higher (minimum of 3 units from Science and 3 units from Arts)  Although it does not count toward the minor, students completing a Psychology minor must also take STATQ 151 . Many 200-level or higher PSYCH courses require STATQ 151 à as a prerequisite so students must plan accordingly.

sections.heading: Notes
sections.content: Biological Sciences courses include ANATE/ANAT, BIOCM/BIOCH, BIOIN, BIOLE/BIOL, BOTQ/BOT, CELL, ENT, GENET, IMINE/MMI, MA SC, MICRE/MICRB , MMI (with the exception of MMI 133 ), NEURO, NU FS 363 , PMCOL (with the exception of PMCOL 300 ), PALEO, PHYSE/PHYSL (with the exception of PHYSL 600 ), ZOOL.  Mathematical Sciences courses include CMPUT, MATHQ/MATH, STATQ/STAT.  Physical Sciences courses include ASTRO, BIOCM/BIOCH, CHIM/CHEM, GEOPH, MA PH, PHYSQ/PHYS.  BIOCM/BIOCH courses may be counted as Biological Sciences or Chemistry.  MA PH courses may be counted as Physical Sciences or Physics.  In addition to the areas and subjects listed above, offered at Faculté Saint-Jean, students may choose a minor from those offered by the Faculty of Science unless there is an equivalent minor offered by Faculté Saint-Jean (Note: Faculty of Science courses are offered in English). Within each of these minors, students must meet the requirements of the Faculty of Science (see Bachelor of Science (Major and Honors) ) as well as those of Faculté Saint- Jean. Also the student may choose a minor in Agriculture, Environmental and Conservation Sciences or Human Ecology offered by the Faculty of Agricultural, Life and Environmental Sciences (see Bachelor of Science General - Minor in Agricultural, Life and Environmental Sciences ). Note that the Faculté residence requirements are applicable in this case (see Residence ).

sections.heading: Non-Science Minors
sections.content: Science students may also complete a minor in Arts.  For the minor concentration in Arts, students must complete a minimum of 15 units at the senior level, including at least 6 units at the 300- or 400-level, and a maximum of 24 units.  For the minor concentration in Music, the student must complete a minimum of 12 units at the senior level, including at least 6 units at the 300- or 400-level, and a maximum of 24 units.  For information about the BSc General – minor in Arts, see BA Major/Minor Requirements.  For information about the BSc General – minor in Agricultural, Life and Environmental Sciences, see BSc General—Minor in Agricultural, Life and Environmental Sciences. In all cases, the faculty and/or department-specified requirements for the minor must be met.  In addition to the areas listed above and offered at Faculté Saint-Jean, students may choose a minor from those offered by the Faculty of Arts unless there is an equivalent minor offered by Faculté Saint-Jean (Note: Faculty of Arts courses are offered in English). Within each of these minors, students must meet the requirements of the Faculty of Arts as well as those of Faculté Saint-Jean. Note that the Faculté residence requirements are applicable in this case (see Residence ).

sections.heading: Bachelor of Education/Bachelor of Science Degrees
sections.content: The Faculté Saint-Jean offers a combined degree program that allows students to obtain two degrees (one in science and one in education).  Bachelor of Education/Bachelor of Science Combined Degrees Program

sections.heading: Academic Standing and Graduation
sections.content: To obtain a BSc General degree, students must maintain a GPA of 2.0 in 120 units credited to the degree. A GPA of at least 2.3 is required in the major subject or area of concentration.

Bachelor of Education/Bachelor of Science Combined Degrees Program

sections.heading: General Information
sections.content: Students planning to apply for admission to the Honors program should consult BA Honors for admission requirements.  See Bachelor of Arts Honors for Faculty regulations concerning the Honors program.  The BA Honors in Mathematics is a flexible program. Students can design the degree for a general liberal education or for a specific career. The Department of Mathematical and Statistical Sciences also offers various BSc programs with Honors in Mathematics (see BSc Subject Areas ).

sections.heading: Program Requirements
sections.content: Honors in Mathematics requires a minimum of 51 and a maximum of 57 units as follows.

sections.heading: Course Requirements
sections.content: MATH 117 - Honors Calculus I  MATH 118 - Honors Calculus II  MATH 127 - Honors Linear Algebra I

sections.heading: Promotion Requirements
sections.content: Promotion from year to year requires an overall GPA of at least 3.0 with a GPA of at least 3.3 in all MATH courses in the Fall/Winter.

sections.heading: Graduation Requirements
sections.content: Graduation with Honors in Mathematics requires a graduation average of at least 3.0 with an average of at least 3.3 in all MATH courses. Graduation with First Class Honors requires an average of at least 3.7 on all MATH courses taken in the last two years and an average of at least 3.5 or better on all courses in the two final years (last 60 units).

sections.heading: STAT 337 - Biostatistics
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-2  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: first term  Methods of data analysis useful in Biostatistics including analysis of variance and covariance and nested designs, multiple regression, logistic regression and log-linear models. The concepts will be motivated by problems in the life sciences. Applications to real data will be emphasized through the use of a computer package. Prerequisite: STAT 151, STAT 161, or SCI 151 and a 200-level Biological Science course. Notes : (1) Credit can be obtained in at most one of STAT 252, STAT 337, and AREC 313. (2) This course may not be taken for credit if credit has already been obtained in STAT 368 or 378.

title: STAT 181 - Introduction to Combinatorics and Probability

sections.heading: STAT 181 - Introduction to Combinatorics and Probability
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Induction; principles of counting, multinomial coefficients, negative binomial distribution; maximum likelihood estimation, probability axioms; conditional probability, Bayes’ rule; independence; probability mass, distribution, and moment generating functions; strong law of large numbers; conditional expectation estimators; gambler’s ruin; transience and recurrence; compound processes; applications. Corequisite: One of MATH 101, 118, 136, 146, or 156. Prerequisite: One of MATH 125 or 127. Notes: (1) Credit can be obtained in at most two of STAT 181, STAT 265, or STAT 281. (2) Credit cannot be obtained in STAT 181 if credit has already been obtained in MATH 181.

sections.heading: STAT 235 - Introductory Statistics for Engineering
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3.8  Approved Hours: 3-0-1.5  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term or Spring/Summer  Descriptive data analysis. Calculus of Probability. Binomial, normal, and exponential distributions. Sampling distributions. Estimation, testing hypotheses, and one-way analysis of variance. Linear correlation and regression. Sampling. Use of a microcomputer software package for statistical analyses in engineering applications. Prerequisite: MATH 100. Corequisite: MATH 101. Notes: (1) This course may not be taken for credit if credit has already been obtained in one of STAT 151, 161, 222, 265, 266, 276, 281; KIN 109, PEDS 109, PSYCH 211, PTHER 352, SCI 151 or SOC 210. (2) Intended for Engineering students. (2) Intended for Engineering students. Other students who take this course will receive 3.0 units.

sections.heading: STAT 276 - Statistics for Data Science
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Fundamental principles of statistical learning and inference for data science Understanding of types of analytics, probability, variability, relationship between variables, probability distributions, law of large numbers, Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing and statistical significance, and elementary theory of regression. Prerequisite: STAT 281 or STAT 265. Students presenting STAT 265 must also present one of MATH 117 or MATH 216 as corequisite. Credit can only be obtained in one of STAT 266 or STAT 276.

title: STAT 281 - Probability by Counting and Queuing

sections.heading: STAT 281 - Probability by Counting and Queuing
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Review of binomial and negative binomial distributions; continuous random variables; uniform, exponential, and gamma distributions; conditional probability; properties of conditional expectation; stochastic processes; finite-dimensional distributions, Poisson approximation; Poisson measures; counting processes, Markov queues, customer time in queues; steady-state distributions; applications. Corequisite: One of MATH 209, 214, or 217. Notes: (1) Credit can be obtained in at most two of STAT 181, STAT 265, or STAT 281. (2) Credit cannot be obtained in STAT 281 if credit has already been obtained in STAT 371.

sections.heading: STAT 353 - Life Contingencies I
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Time at death random variables, continuous and discrete insurances, endowments and varying annuities, net premiums and reserves. Prerequisites: MATH 253 and one of STAT 265 or STAT 281.

sections.heading: STAT 361 - Sampling Techniques
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Simple random sampling from finite populations, stratified sampling, regression estimators, cluster sampling. Prerequisite: One of STAT 266 or STAT 276, or STAT 235 with consent of the Department. Note: This course may only be offered in alternate years.

title: STAT 368 - Introduction to Design and Analysis of Experiments

sections.heading: STAT 368 - Introduction to Design and Analysis of Experiments
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Basic principles of experimental design, completely randomized design-one way ANOVA and ANCOVA, randomized block design, Latin square design, Multiple comparisons. Nested designs. Factorial experiments. Prerequisite: One of STAT 266 or STAT 276, or STAT 235 with consent of the Department.

sections.heading: STAT 371 - Probability and Stochastic Processes
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Problem solving of classical probability questions, random walk, gambler’s ruin, Markov chains, branching processes. Selected topics of the instructor’s choice. Prerequisite: STAT 265. Note: Credit can be obtained in at most one of STAT 281 or STAT 371.

sections.heading: STAT 372 - Mathematical Statistics
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Laws of large numbers, weak convergence, some asymptotic results, delta method, maximum likelihood estimation, testing, UMP tests, LR tests, nonparametric methods (sign test, rank test), robustness, statistics and their sensitivity properties, prior and posterior distributions, Bayesian inference, conjugate priors, Bayes estimators. Prerequisites: STAT 266 or STAT 276.

sections.heading: STAT 378 - Applied Regression Analysis
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Simple linear regression analysis, inference on regression parameters, residual analysis, prediction intervals, weighted least squares. Multiple regression analysis, inference about regression parameters, multicollinearity and its effects, indicator variables, selection of independent variables. Non-linear regression. Prerequisite: One of STAT 266 or STAT 276, or STAT 235 with consent of the Department.

sections.heading: STAT 413 - Computing for Data Science
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: second term  Survey of contemporary languages/environments suitable for algorithms of Statistics and Data Science. Introduction to Monte Carlo methods, random number generation and numerical integration in statistical context and optimization for both smooth and constrained alternatives, tailored to specific applications in statistics and machine learning. Prerequisites: One of STAT 265 or STAT 281, or consent of the Department.

sections.heading: STAT 432 - Survival Analysis
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Survival models, model estimation from complete and incomplete data samples, parametric survival models with concomitant variables, estimation of life tables from general population data. Prerequisites: STAT 372 and 378.

sections.heading: STAT 437 - Applied Statistical Methods
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Methods of data analysis useful in applied research, including repeated measures and longitudinal data analysis, non-linear regression, survival analysis, multivariate techniques. Applications to real data will be emphasized, including case studies and real data applications. Each researcher works on a project to present, highlighting the methods used in the project. Prerequisite: STAT 252 or 337 or consent of the instructor.

title: STAT 441 - Statistical Methods for Learning and Data Mining

sections.heading: STAT 441 - Statistical Methods for Learning and Data Mining
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Review of linear and nonlinear regression and brief introduction to generalized linear models, the course covers selected methods of dimension reduction (principal components, factor analysis, canonical correlations), of unsupervised (clustering, multidimensional scaling ordination) and supervised classification (discriminant analysis, logistic regression, nearest neighbours - including, among others, the machine learning methods like classification trees, neural networks, and support vector machines). Prerequisite: STAT 378.

sections.heading: STAT 453 - Risk Theory
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Classical ruin theory, individual risk models, collective risk models, models for loss severity: parametric models, tail behavior, models for loss frequency, mixed Poisson models; compound Poisson models, convolutions and recursive methods, probability and moment generating functions. Prerequisite: One of STAT 371 or STAT 281.

sections.heading: STAT 471 - Probability I
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Probability spaces, algebra of events. Elements of combinatorial analysis. Conditional probability, stochastic independence. Special discrete and continuous distributions. Random variables, moments, transformations. Basic limit theorems. Prerequisite: One of STAT 371 or STAT 281.

sections.heading: STAT 479 - Time Series Analysis
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Stationary series, spectral analysis, models in time series: autoregressive, moving average, ARMA and ARIMA. Smoothing series, computational techniques and computer packages for time series. Prerequisites: STAT 372 and 378. Note: This course may only be offered in alternate years.

sections.heading: STAT 497 - Reading in Statistics
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  This course is designed to give credit to mature and able students for reading in areas not covered by courses, under the supervision of a staff member. A student, or group of students, wishing to use this course should find a staff member willing to supervise the proposed reading program. A detailed description of the material to be covered should be submitted to the Chair of the Department Honors Committee. (This should include a description of testing methods to be used.) The program will require the approval of both the Honors Committee, and the Chair of the Department. The students’ mastery of the material of the course will be tested by a written or oral examination. This course may be taken in Fall or Winter and may be taken any number of times, subject always to the approval mentioned above. Prerequisite: Any 300-level STAT course.

title: STAT 498 - Statistical Topics in Data Science

sections.heading: STAT 498 - Statistical Topics in Data Science
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  This topics course is designed for new course offerings that may be offered in a given term. Prerequisites: One of STAT 266 or 276. Additional prerequisites may be required. Note: Credit for this course may be obtained more than once.

sections.heading: STAT 499 - Research Project
sections.content: Course Career: Undergraduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 0-1S-6  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  This course provides students in Major, Specialization and Honors programs an opportunity to pursue research in statistics under the direction of a member of the Department. Course requirements include at least one oral presentation and a written final report. Students interested in taking this course should contact the course coordinator two months in advance. Credit for this course may be obtained more than once. Prerequisites: a 300-level STAT course and consent of the course coordinator.

sections.heading: STAT 501 - Directed Study I
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Basic principles of experimental design, completely randomized design-one way ANOVA and ANCOVA. Randomized block design. Latin square design, Multiple comparisons. Nested designs. Factorial experiments. Each student will give a written report and seminar presentation highlighting statistical methods used in a research project. Prerequisites: STAT 252 or 337 or equivalent and a course in linear algebra. Note: Cannot be used for credit towards a graduate program in Statistics.

sections.heading: STAT 502 - Directed Study II
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Simple linear regression analysis, inference on regression parameters, residual analysis, prediction intervals, weighted least squares. Multiple regression analysis, inference about regression parameters, multicollinearity and its effects, indicator variables, selection of independent variables. Non-linear regression. Each student will give a written report and seminar presentation highlighting statistical methods used in a research project. Prerequisite: STAT 337 or equivalent and a course in linear algebra. Note: Cannot be used for credit towards a graduate program in Statistics.

sections.heading: STAT 503 - Directed Study III
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Theory and applications of time series modelling, stationarity, autocorrelation. Spectral properties, filtering. Box-Jenkins models, seasonality. Each student will give a written report and seminar presentation highlighting statistical methods used in a research project. Prerequisite: STAT 372 and 378 or consent of Instructor.

sections.heading: STAT 504 - Directed Study IV
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Basic sampling schemes for finite populations: simple random sampling, stratified random sampling, systematic sampling and cluster sampling. Unequal probability sampling. Ratio and regression estimators. Prerequisite: A course in Statistical Inference at the 300 level or permission from the instructor. Note: Cannot be used for credit towards a graduate program in Statistics.

sections.heading: STAT 505 - Directed Study V
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Principles of statistical model building and analysis applied in linear and generalized linear models and illustrated through multivariate methods such as repeated measures, principal components, and supervised and unsupervised classification. Each student will give a written report and seminar presentation highlighting statistical methods used in a research project. Prerequisites: STAT 501, 502 or equivalent. Note: Cannot be used for credit towards a thesis-based graduate program in Statistics.

title: STAT 512 - Techniques of Mathematics for Statistics

sections.heading: STAT 512 - Techniques of Mathematics for Statistics
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Introduction to mathematical techniques commonly used in theoretical Statistics, with applications. Applications of diagonalization results for real symmetric matrices, and of continuity, differentiation, Riemann-Stieltjes integration and multivariable calculus to the theory of Statistics including least squares estimation, generating functions, distribution theory. Prerequisite: consent of Department.

sections.heading: STAT 513 - Statistical Computing
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Introduction to contemporary computational culture: reproducible coding, literate programming. Monte Carlo methods: random number generation, variance reduction, numerical integration, statistical simulations. Optimization (linear search, gradient descent, Newton-Raphson, method of scoring, and their specifics in the statistical context), EM algorithm. Fundamentals of convex optimization with constraints. Prerequisites: consent of the instructor.

title: STAT 514 - Statistics for Clinical Trials I

sections.heading: STAT 514 - Statistics for Clinical Trials I
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: first term  This course is designed to equip students with essential statistical knowledge and skills necessary for the successful clinical trial design and analysis. This course covers a wide range of statistical topics specific to clinical trials, including intention-to-treat versus efficacy trials, principles of sampling and exclusion, methods of allocation and techniques of randomization, parallel versus cross over design, cluster randomization designs, statistical analysis planning, external and internal validation, and reports of statistical findings. Additionally, the course will explore other selected topics related to logistical issues in the management of clinical trials. Prerequisite: consent of the instructor. Notes: Students outside of the course-based MSc with a specialization in Biostatistics need permission from the Department to enroll in this course. Thesis-based graduate students in Mathematical and Statistical Sciences cannot take this course for credit.

title: STAT 515 - Statistics for Clinical Trials II

sections.heading: STAT 515 - Statistics for Clinical Trials II
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: second term  This course is a continuation of Statistics for Clinical Trials I, with a focus on statistical computation and data analysis techniques specifically tailored for clinical trials. Students will work with the R and SAS statistical programming languages to gain a comprehensive understanding of these methods in the clinical trials context. The primary goal is to equip graduate students with the statistical skills required for data analysis in clinical trials. Successful students will become proficient in using statistical computational tools to analyze real-world clinical datasets and will be exposed to advanced statistical techniques and best practices for data storage, management, and analysis. Key statistical topics covered in this course include sampling designs, chi-square tests, linear models, mixed-effects models for repeated measurements and survival analysis. Prerequisite: STAT 514. Notes: Students outside of the course-based MSc with a specialization in Biostatistics need permission from the Department to enroll in this course. Thesis-based graduate students in Mathematical and Statistical Sciences cannot take this course for credit.

sections.heading: STAT 532 - Survival Analysis
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Survival and hazard functions, censoring, truncation. Non-parametric, parametric and semi-parametric approaches to survival analysis including Kaplan-Meier estimation and Cox’s proportional hazards model. Prerequisite: STAT 372 or consent of Department.

title: STAT 537 - Statistical Methods for Applied Research II

sections.heading: STAT 537 - Statistical Methods for Applied Research II
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Review of basic statistical concepts of inference and probability theory. Includes applied methods of Linear and non-linear regression and analysis of variance for designed experiments, multiple comparisons, correlations, modeling and variable selection, multicollinearity, predictions, confounding and Simpson’s paradox. Includes case studies and real data applications. Each researcher works on a project to present, highlighting the methods used in the project. Prerequisite: STAT 437 equivalent or consent of the instructor.

sections.heading: STAT 541 - Statistics for Learning
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  The course focuses on statistical learning techniques, in particular those of supervised classification, both from statistical (logistic regression, discriminant analysis, nearest neighbours, and others) and “machine learning” background (tree-based methods, neural networks, support vector machines), with the emphasis on decision-theoretic underpinnings and other statistical aspects, flexible model building (regularization with penalties), and algorithmic solutions. Selected methods of unsupervised classification (clustering) and some related regression methods are covered as well. Prerequisite: Consent of the instructor.

sections.heading: STAT 553 - Risk Theory
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Classical ruin theory, individual risk models, collective risk models, models for loss severity: parametric models, tail behavior, models for loss frequency, mixed Poisson models; compound Poisson models, convolutions and recursive methods, probability and moment generating functions. Prerequisite: STAT 371 or equivalent. Note: Cannot be used for credit towards a thesis-based graduate program in the Department of Mathematical and Statistical Sciences.

sections.heading: STAT 561 - Sample Survey Methodology
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Review of basic sampling schemes: simple random sampling, and stratified random sampling, and systematic sampling. Multistage sampling schemes. Estimation of nonlinear parameters: ratios, regression coefficients, and correlation coefficients. Variance estimation techniques: linearization, BRR, jackknife, and bootstrap. Selected topics: model-based estimation, regression analysis from complex survey data. Relevant computer packages. Prerequisites: STAT 361, 372, 471.

sections.heading: STAT 562 - Discrete Data Analysis
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Sampling models and methods of inference for discrete data. Maximum likelihood estimation for complete contingency tables, measures of association and agreement. Goodness-of-fit. Incomplete tables. Analysis of square tables; symmetry and marginal homogeneity. Model selection and closeness of fit; practical aspects. Chi-square tests for categorical data from complex surveys. Prerequisite: STAT 372 or 471.

sections.heading: STAT 566 - Methods of Statistical Inference
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  An introduction to the theory of statistical inference. Topics to include exponential families and general linear models, likelihood, sufficiency, ancillarity, interval and point estimation, asymptotic approximations. Optional topics as time allows, may include Bayesian methods, Robustness, resampling techniques. This course is intended primarily for MSc students. Prerequisite: STAT 471 or consent of Department.

title: STAT 568 - Design and Analysis of Experiments

sections.heading: STAT 568 - Design and Analysis of Experiments
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  The general linear model. Fully randomized designs, one-way layout, multiple comparisons. Block designs, Latin squares. Factorial designs confounding, fractions. Nested designs, randomization restrictions. Response surface methodology. Analysis of covariance. Prerequisite: STAT 368 and a 400-level STAT course.

sections.heading: STAT 571 - Probability and Measure
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Measure and integration, Laws of Large Numbers, convergence of probability measures. Conditional expectation as time permits. Prerequisites: STAT 471 and STAT 512 or their equivalents.

sections.heading: STAT 575 - Multivariate Analysis
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  The multivariate normal distribution, multivariate regression and analysis of variance, classification, canonical correlation, principal components, factor analysis. Prerequisite: STAT 372 and STAT 512.

sections.heading: STAT 578 - Regression Analysis
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Multiple linear regression, ordinary and generalized least squares, partial and multiple correlation. Regression diagnostics, collinearity, model building. Nonlinear regression. Selected topics: robust and nonparametric regression, measurement error models. Prerequisites: STAT 378 and a 400-level statistics course.

sections.heading: STAT 580 - Stochastic Processes
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Elements of stochastic processes. Discrete and continuous time Markov Chains; Birth and Death processes. Branching processes. Brownian Motion. General Stationary and Markov processes. Examples. Prerequisite: STAT 471 or consent of Instructor.

sections.heading: STAT 590 - Statistical Consulting
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Data analysis, problem solving, oral communication with clients, issues in planning experiments and collecting data; practical aspects of consulting and report writing. Corequisite: STAT 568 and 578 or their equivalents.

sections.heading: STAT 600 - Reading in Statistics
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Students will be supervised by an individual staff member to participate in areas of research interest of that staff member. Students can register only with the permission of the Chair of the Department in special circumstances. Will not be counted toward the minimum course requirement for graduate credits.

title: STAT 637 - Statistical Methods for Applied Research III

sections.heading: STAT 637 - Statistical Methods for Applied Research III
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Advanced statistical design and methods. Topics include sampling, multivariate techniques, survival analysis, power and sample size, linear and non linear regression, longitudinal and repeated measures data, and analysis of seasonal or time series data. Topics to be covered can be influenced by the registered researchers. Includes critical review and case studies with real data applications. Each researcher works on a project to present, highlighting the methods used in the project. Prerequisite: STAT 537 or equivalent or consent of the instructor. Cannot be used for credit towards a graduate program in Statistics.

sections.heading: STAT 664 - Advanced Statistical Inference
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Modern methods of statistical inference. Various versions of likelihood: conditional, marginal, integrated, profile, partial, empirical. Estimating equations. Semi-parametric models. Foundational issues. Prerequisites: STAT 512 and 566.

title: STAT 665 - Asymptotic Methods in Statistical Inference

sections.heading: STAT 665 - Asymptotic Methods in Statistical Inference
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 3  Approved Hours: 3-0-0  Fee index: 6  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Approximation techniques and asymptotic methods in statistics. Topics may include second and higher order expansions, asymptotics of likelihood based estimation and testing. Edgeworth expansions, exponential tilting, asymptotic relative efficiency, U-, M-, L-, and R-estimation. Prerequisites: STAT 566 or 664 and 512 or the equivalent.

sections.heading: STAT 900 - Directed Research Project
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 6  Approved Hours: UNASSIGNED  Fee index: 12  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: variable  Open only to students taking the MSc non-thesis option in statistics.

sections.heading: STAT 901 - Practicum in Statistics I
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 1.5  Approved Hours: UNASSIGNED  Fee index: 3  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Open only to students taking the MSc non-thesis option in Statistics.

sections.heading: STAT 902 - Practicum in Statistics II
sections.content: Course Career: Graduate  Units: 1.5  Approved Hours: UNASSIGNED  Fee index: 3  Faculty: Science  Department: Mathematical & Statistical Sci  Typically Offered: either term  Open only to students taking the MSc non-thesis option in Statistics.

sections.heading: STAT 903 - Internship in Biostatistics
